data.title: PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The S&P Global Infrastructure Index advanced 2.2% in the fourth quarter. Benchmark performance was led higher by solid performance in the utility and transportation infrastructure sectors. The midstream energy segment was the only notable area of index weakness during the period.

Quarter

The PGIM Jennison Global Infrastructure Fund advanced but modestly underperformed the S&P Global Infrastructure Index for the quarter.

In absolute terms, the Fund was led by strong performance from Ferrovial (FER), selected names in the Utility sector (GREENERGY RENOVABLES, Elia Group, Italgas) and the transportation company Group Aeroportuario del Pacifico. A number of Utilities also underperformed during the period, such as Vistra, Southern Company (SO), Xcel Energy (XEL), as did Cheniere Energy (LNG).

Relative to the S&P Global Infrastructure Index, the Fund's positioning in transportation infrastructure detracted the most from relative results. In addition, out of index exposure to communications and information technology hurt relative performance. On the upside, the Fund's positioning in midstream energy and utilities benefited relative performance during the period.

Key Contributors

  • Ferrovial
  • Grenergy Renovables
  • Elia Group

Ferrovial , the Spain-based infrastructure and construction company, maintained its strong momentum through the fourth quarter, supported by resilient performance across its core toll road portfolio and continued strength in North American traffic volumes. Revenue growth remained robust, aided by a strong double digit price increase announced for its primary Canadian concession in 2026. Additionally, Ferrovial will be included in the Nasdaq 100 adding to buying momentum in the name.

Grenergy Renovables , a Madrid-based renewable energy company, was a notable contributor during the quarter as investor sentiment toward European renewable energy operators improved following easing bond yields and supportive regulatory developments. The company advanced several key solar and wind projects to late-stage development and reported stable power purchase agreement pricing, which bolstered revenue visibility heading into 2026. Operational execution across its energy generation and services segments contributed to earnings resilience despite macroeconomic volatility.

Elia Group operates as a leading European transmission system operator in Belgium and Germany. The company continued to deliver sector-leading growth during the fourth quarter, with ongoing investment in cross-border grid expansion and offshore wind interconnections. Execution on its multi-year capital plan remained on track, and regulatory clarity around cost recovery further strengthened investor confidence. The stock performed well as markets recognized the growing importance of regulated transmission infrastructure in enabling Europe's energy transition. With a proven track record of delivering complex grid projects and a visible growth pipeline, Elia remains well positioned as a high-quality compounder within the regulated utility space.

Key Detractors

  • Cheniere Energy
  • Vistra Corp. (VST)
  • NEXTDC Limited (NXDCF)

Cheniere Energy is an integrated, full-service liquefied natural gas (LNG) company that owns and operates LNG receiving terminals and pipelines in Louisiana and Texas. Shares lagged during the fourth quarter as natural gas prices softened and investor sentiment across the broader energy complex weakened amid milder winter weather forecasts and elevated storage levels. Although quarterly results remained broadly in line with expectations, the company's reaffirmed full-year guidance and strong long-term contract portfolio offered limited offset to near-term commodity-driven volatility. The market's focus on declining spot margins overshadowed continued expansion progress at the Corpus Christi Stage 3 project. We continue to view the company's extensive, long-dated contract base as a durable cash flow anchor supporting long-term value creation through the next LNG investment cycle.

Vistra Corp. is an independent power producer operating an integrated retail and electric power generation business primarily in markets throughout the U.S. Shares underperformed in the quarter as sentiment within independent power producers softened, driven by investor caution around the timing of data center procurement activity and near-term power market volatility. While earnings and free cash flow guidance remained consistent with expectations, the stock experienced pressure following reduced forward price expectations across select regional power markets. Despite these headwinds, Vistra's diversified generation portfolio, continued strength in retail load, and exposure to structural Artificial Intelligence (AI)-driven electricity demand underpin its long-term growth profile.

NEXTDC Limited develops and operates hyperscale data centers across Australia and the Asia-Pacific region. Shares detracted from performance as the regional technology sector experienced valuation compression amid macroeconomic pressures and cautious investor sentiment toward capital-intensive digital infrastructure. Despite reporting strong operational metrics, including record utilization rates and continued pre-leasing momentum, rising financing costs weighed on near-term equity performance. The company remains well positioned to benefit from sustained hyperscale and cloud spending across the Asia-Pacific region, and its strategic focus on energy-efficient, renewable-backed data center design aligns closely with structural themes driving long-term digital infrastructure demand.

Portfolio Outlook

Overall, listed infrastructure stocks declined modestly through December 2025, though the asset class continued to outperform the broader markets as its defensive characteristics offset a weak market backdrop. The strategic importance of reliable baseload power capacity remained a central theme as hyperscale data center projects continued to advance, but investors rotated selectively within the universe following a strong year-to-date run in several power-related segments. Electric utility and power generation exposures remained broadly resilient relative to benchmarks, supported by structural demand growth and differentiated positioning in grid-constrained regions, while areas tied more directly to macro-sensitive growth and higher-beta risk factors experienced greater valuation pressure.

European infrastructure shares remained comparatively resilient in December, aided by supportive regulatory frameworks and continued visibility on long-term capital investment programs. Construction and engineering-focused names benefitted from solid backlogs and disciplined project execution, even as new order momentum moderated amid softer macro data. Regulated utilities in the region again provided ballast to returns, with transmission and multi-utility operators outperforming their global peers thanks to strong balance sheets and clear pathways for grid modernization and renewable integration. Transport infrastructure performance remained mixed; toll roads and rail assets delivered steady cash flows, while airport operators faced persistent cost pressures and uneven passenger mix recovery.

In the United States, power generation and utility sectors underperformed in absolute terms during December but continued to fare better than the broader infrastructure complex as the “AI power” theme shifted from multiple expansion toward an earnings- and project-execution-driven phase. The structural step-up in electricity demand remained intact, yet profit-taking and increased scrutiny of project timing, regulatory approvals, and customer cost pass-through introduced greater share price volatility. Transmission-focused and grid-centric business models were generally favored over more complex vertically integrated structures facing heightened regulatory and political attention around affordability and reliability.

North American natural gas infrastructure remained in recalibration mode as markets reassessed medium-term demand and capacity needs following a volatile year for commodity prices and policy debate. Pipeline and midstream systems continued to move robust volumes under long-term contracts, but equity valuations reflected questions about the pace and scale of future expansion projects and the permitting environment for new long-distance infrastructure. Gas-fired generation retained its critical role balancing intermittent renewables and fast-growing data center load, reinforcing the fuel's position as a key component of the evolving power mix even as investors increasingly differentiate among regions and asset quality.

Electric utility and power generation segments experienced some multiple compression into year-end, yet remained supported by visibility on large, regulator-backed capital expenditure plans for transmission, distribution, and interconnection upgrades. Companies with strong balance sheets, proven execution on complex grid projects, and meaningful exposure to high-growth demand corridors continued to be viewed as central beneficiaries of the power infrastructure “super-cycle”. In contrast, communications-oriented infrastructure linked to towers and data centers within the wider listed universe saw persistent rate-sensitive de-rating, even as data traffic and cloud workloads continued to rise.

Transport infrastructure fundamentals stayed broadly intact despite softer macro data and ongoing trade and geopolitical uncertainty. North American toll roads demonstrated solid traffic volumes and pricing power, reflecting resilient commuting and freight patterns, while freight and passenger rail assets benefited from stable long-haul demand and incremental efficiency gains. In Europe, sustained public investment and policy support underpinned long-term capital programs across key corridors, though airport-linked assets remained more volatile due to fluctuating travel patterns, labor constraints, and elevated operating costs.

Transport infrastructure fundamentals showed resilient performance despite continued uncertainty from evolving trade policies. North American toll roads continued benefiting from solid traffic volumes and pricing power, while European transport infrastructure companies remained supported by fiscal spending commitments across major economies. Airport operators experienced divergent performance with recovery patterns varying by region and operational focus, though overall trends remain constructive for companies with strong market positions.

Communications infrastructure across global markets continued to contend with higher real yields and elevated capital intensity, which weighed on valuations for tower and data-center-heavy business models. Nevertheless, structural drivers—including 5G densification, cloud adoption, and AI-related compute requirements—remained firmly in place, pointing to ongoing needs for densified networks and high-availability interconnection hubs. Markets increasingly demanded clearer line-of-sight to free-cash-flow growth, disciplined capital allocation, and potential capital returns before re-rating these assets, leaving the segment's near-term performance more sensitive to macro and policy shifts than other parts of the infrastructure universe.

data.title: Retreating But Not Defeated: AUD/USD Bulls Find Hope In Technical Support And Hawkish RBA
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

AUD/USD fell 1%+ on Thursday, with the pair reacting to a resurgent US dollar as safe haven demand intensified due to Middle East tensions. AUD/USD was trading at a multi-year peak, and thus a retracement may be welcomed in some quarters.

Hawkish RBA may limit downside

Inflationary pressures are intensifying in Australia, where the Melbourne Institute survey recently reported that March Consumer Inflation Expectations climbed to 5.2%, the highest level since July 2023.

This uptick from February's 5% reading underscores the growing challenge for the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA), which had already taken a proactive stance in early February by raising the Official Cash Rate (OCR) by 25 basis points to 3.85%.

As the ongoing energy crisis continues to drive costs higher, market speculation is mounting that the RBA and other global central banks will be forced to maintain an aggressive interest rate hiking cycle to contain burgeoning prices.

According to the latest LSEG data, markets are pricing in around a 78% probability of a 25 bps rate hike at the upcoming March 17, 2026 meeting.

Economic data ahead

While the Australian economic calendar remains quiet this Friday, the focus shifts to a heavy slate of data from the United States. Markets are bracing for a series of high-impact releases, including the January Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index, the Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation metric, alongside January Durable Goods Orders.

Additionally, the preliminary March reading of the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index will provide a fresh look at how the spike in energy prices and financial market volatility are impacting American consumer confidence.

Markets may remain at the mercy of geopolitical developments, which could even overshadow tomorrow's US data. The intriguing part about AUD/USD is the technical picture, where the pair is at a key inflection point.

Technical Analysis - AUD/USD

From a technical point of view, AUD/USD has finally pulled back from its multi-year peak around the 0.7187 handle. The pullback has brought the pair to a key inflection area resting between the 0.07072 (100-day MA) and 0.7051 (200-day MA) handles.

The previous impulse move to the upside occurred after a breakout of the 0.7034 handle, which rests just below the inflection area.

AUD/USD Four-Hour Chart, March 13, 2026

Dropping down to a one-hour chart, there is some evidence that bulls may already be returning. The price has slowed significantly as it approached the 0.7070 handle.

The bigger hint comes from the period-14 RSI, which has just left oversold territory. This is usually seen as a sign that momentum has shifted in favor of the bulls.

An immediate hurdle on the H1 chart rests at 0.7100. Acceptance back above this level is needed before the 0.7130 and recent highs at 0.7187 come into focus.

data.title: Harbor Diversified International All Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
We are beginning to see signs that the unprecedented global tightening cycle of the past few years is impacting companies. - Marathon London

Market in Review

The fourth quarter started with relative calm, as most global markets trended sideways until the final week of November. In the previous week, a downswing had been in place, but the trend reversed — and the upswing — comparatively gentle though it was, continued unchecked into year-end. All regional MSCI indexes that Marathon follows ended the period ahead, with the MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) returning 3.3%. For the first time in several years, the U.S. market was a relative laggard; the regions were led by Europe, with the MSCI Europe Index generating a return of 6.2%.

The quarter's initial uncertainty was caused, at least partly, by the U.S. government shutdown, which occurred from the first day of the period until November 12. This event led to the closure of various government departments that provide economic data upon which many investors base their trading decisions. Following the resumption of services, markets began to worry that data would not be reliable enough to support their positions, and the market declined until the first official releases showed a benign picture, allowing the U.S. Federal Reserve to cut rates twice during the quarter.

By style, quality outperformed both value and growth globally. Regionally, that was only true in the U.S. In all other regions value outperformed growth by between 3% and 6%. By size, large-cap stocks outperformed in all regions.

Retirement Class shares commenced operations on March 1, 2016. The performance attributed to the Retirement Class shares prior to that date is that of the Institutional Class shares. Performance prior to March 1, 2016 has not been adjusted to reflect the lower expenses of Retirement Class shares. During this period, Retirement Class shares would have had returns similar to, but somewhat higher than, Institutional Class shares due to the fact that Retirement Class shares represent interests in the same portfolio as Institutional Class shares but are subject to lower expenses.

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expenses.

The net expense ratios for this fund are subject to a contractual management fee waiver and/or expense limitation agreement, excluding interest expense and acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), through 02/28/2026.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor Diversified International All Cap Fund (Institutional Class, "Fund") returned 3.31%, underperforming its benchmark, the MSCI All Country World Ex.-U.S. (ND) Index, which returned 5.05%. We believe the Fund's underperformance was a result of stock selection in Europe, though it also lagged slightly in the Pacific and Canada.

From a sector perspective, underperformance was equally split between stock selection and asset allocation, which is a result of stock selection decisions rather than a separate decision within the investment process.

Stock selection was strong in Materials but lagged in Industrials and Health Care. Similarly, allocation impacts were generally minor, but the underweight to Information Technology was materially negative. Only the underweight to Communication Services partially mitigated the impact. From a style perspective, value outperformed growth, which was beneficial in the Pacific, but less so elsewhere. Large-cap stocks outperformed small- and mid-cap stocks.

Contributors & Detractors

Leading detractors during the quarter included 3i Group (IIIYY), Roche (RHHBY), (not holding) SK Hynix (HXSCL), Tencent Music Entertainment (TME), and Naspers.

In November, 3i Group reported earnings in line with expectations, but warned that the outlook for key markets — particularly France, 3i's subsidiary Action's largest market — was deteriorating, which triggered a sharp sell-off; the stock fell around 25% from its previous quarterly peak.

Roche outperformed during the quarter, thus the Fund's underweight to the stock was a negative for relative performance.

Korea's SK Hynix, a semiconductor company with a focus on high-speed memory, soared amid demand from AI data centers. Our lack of exposure, therefore, negatively impacted relative performance.

The Chinese market fell sharply during the quarter, thus holding Tencent (TCEHY) Music (a listed subsidiary of Tencent) was a negative. Similarly, South Africa-listed Naspers, which is an investor in a variety of technology companies and notably the largest indirect holder of Tencent shares, suffered.

Top contributors during the quarter included Barclays (BCS), Standard Chartered (SCBFY), Capitec Bank, and (not holding) Alibaba Group (BABA) and Tencent Holdings.

Positions in the U.K.'s Barclays and Standard Chartered appreciated as investors rotated into value stocks. Despite strong gains in recent years, European banks remain conservatively valued versus many non-European peers.

South Africa's Capitec Bank reported a 26% increase in earnings (local currency terms) on the first day of the quarter, driven by an expansion of its banking client base, insurance business, and fintech arm.

The Chinese market fell sharply, led by technology and electric vehicle stocks. The Fund's lack of exposure to social media giant Tencent and online retailer Alibaba thus benefited performance.

Buys and Sells

The Fund acquired Iberdrola (IBDRY), Scout24 (SCOTY), and Empresa Nacional de Telecomunicaciones (ENTUY).

Spain's Iberdrola, a leading electric utility, is diversified across the U.S., U.K., Spain, and Brazil. The firm owns natural monopoly assets in networks where new capital cannot enter due to regulation.

Scout24, Germany's leading online property portal, is a technology-led firm with recent double-digit growth. After a rally earlier in the year, shares were down 30% from their high at the time of purchase, presenting Marathon with an attractive entry point.

Empresa Nacional de Telecomunicaciones offers telecommunications services to corporate clients in Chile. Forthcoming consolidation creates scope and the opportunity for pricing power to grow.

We exited positions in Amrize, M3, and Gruma (GPAGY).

Amrize, a holding inherited from a spin-off from Swiss stock Holcim (HCMLY) in June, was sold. The firm's revenue is derived mostly from the U.S., and the stock was moved to the U.S. index in November.

M3 supplies online medical information services for doctors and supports marketing of pharmaceutical companies and medical equipment manufacturers. The stock outperformed meaningfully, and the valuation had risen significantly over the past year until the time of Marathon's exit.

Gruma is engaged primarily in the production, marketing, distribution, and sale of tortillas, corn flour, and wheat flour. The tortilla industry capital cycle appears to be deteriorating with an increasingly cost-conscious consumer down-trading to private labels from core brands like Gruma, resulting in an increasingly aggressive pricing environment.

Overweights and Underweights

The Fund's sector weightings are a by-product of Marathon London's bottom-up stock selection process and do not change materially over the course of a quarter or year.

As of December 31, 2025, Industrials and Consumer Staples were the largest sector overweights, sitting at 5.5% and 2.7%, respectively; Information Technology was still the largest underweight at -6.4%, now followed by Health Care at -3.3%.

Country Allocations

The Fund's exposure to developing markets has not changed materially during the quarter. As of December 31, 2025, the U.K. and South Africa remained the most overweight positions at 8.5% and 3.5%, respectively; China is still the largest underweight at -4.0%, now followed by Switzerland at -4.0%.

Outlook

Marathon London's approach is long term, with a typical target holding period of more than five years. Portfolio managers do not take short-term positions, and Marathon London does not undertake any macroeconomic research, have "house views," or make any such predictions or forecasts.

That said, one interesting observation has been the effect of the extended ultra-low interest rates on Marathon London's approach. Capital cycle analysis seeks opportunities both among high return-on-capital companies, benefiting from a positive ("top half") capital cycle, and those in a lower-return ("bottom half") universe. Usually, the bottom half offers opportunities for companies that must consolidate to improve profitability. However, there has been a dearth of opportunity in the bottom half, because very low interest rates have allowed "zombie" companies to borrow at minimal cost and continue operating. The rise in interest rates since 2021–2022 has incrementally generated some of the "creative destruction," which has been lacking in markets since the Global Financial Crisis and creates bottom-half opportunities for Marathon London. However, the lag between interest rate changes and economic impact varies and can be several years. We are beginning to see signs that the unprecedented global tightening cycle of the past few years is impacting companies. The interest cycle has turned in many countries, however, with rates being cut recently. Nevertheless, due to the lag in impact, we expect to see increased opportunity in the bottom half in the coming years.

Marathon is also cognizant of the currently heightened levels of market volatility. While our long-term approach does not seek to rapidly trade in and out of positions, it is possible that ongoing uncertainty will present opportunities to buy into assets at depressed valuations or sell holdings at high valuations.

Investment Philosophy:

The Harbor Diversified International All Cap Fund invests primarily in common and preferred stocks of foreign companies, including those in emerging markets. The Fund seeks long-term opportunities driven by capital cycles or supply-side conditions, using a fundamental, bottom-up approach. The Fund typically invests in companies across Europe, Japan, the Pacific Basin and emerging markets, and North America. While inherently diversified, a bias towards smaller and mid-cap businesses in niche industries, coupled with a particularly long holding period, result in a portfolio that is significantly differentiated from the Fund's benchmark index.

data.title: USD Extends Its Lead As Fed Cuts Price Out: USD/JPY To New Cycle Highs
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Despite yesterday's more optimistic US CPI report, markets are now focusing on a bleaker inflationary picture ahead. A major theme in markets is the progressive repricing for a more persistent and damaging US-Iran-Israel war, which would have long-lasting consequences on oil prices.

As you can see from the Polymarket odds, the odds for a 5- to 6-week conflict fell from 80% to the current 47%, adding to the (justified) fears of a damaging long war.

With the Iranian regime holding out stronger than expected, nominating a new leader in Mojtaba Khamenei and continuing its attacks on neighboring countries, it seems that it would either require more force or a new plan to really materialize the conflict into the desired regime change outcome.

Participants and the general media are now roasting the Trump Administration for its lack of exit plans. To me, this could be a strategy to create doubt, as we are "only" on the 13th day of the conflict, but people's skepticism regarding Trump's temerity is justified.

Even his prior advisors warned of such, warning of a "self-inflicted wound" to the US economy as oil rallies back to $95.

For those who haven't looked back yet, the consecutive oil crises in the 1970s were the prelude to the infamous stagflation era, and the market never jokes around with such economic trends.

If anybody is enjoying this at least, it's the dollar bulls, as the petrodollar has been fuming higher since the beginning of the conflict. Rate traders have now priced out around 40 bps of rate cut pricing since the beginning of the conflict, with rate cut odds towards the end of 2026 only around 23 bps.

Subject to an almost 1-to-1 correlation with oil since early March, the greenback has kept outperforming major forex currencies, in particular the subject of this afternoon's FX outlook, the yen.

We will dive into a two-timeframe outlook for USD/JPY as the pair comes close to the 160.00 barrier. An intervention could be near!

USD/JPY Daily Chart

USD/JPY has been the most affected major pair since the beginning of the conflict, holding an almost perfectly negative correlation to the oil prices. Asian countries are the major victims of Strait of Hormuz disruptions, particularly concerning oil deliveries.

Despite Japan having some of the most ample strategic oil reserves, the yen was already facing some heavy scrutiny regarding heavy imports and their more elevated costs. The effect of higher forecasted deficits doesn't help. So if the Hormuz Crisis lasts longer, it could be a final nail in the coffin for the pair.

Furthermore, the Japanese yen is highly correlated to US Treasuries, which have been getting ransacked from the higher inflation expectations due to Japan being the No. 1 owner of the debt asset. The combination of both has led to the current 4.60% move higher in the major pair.

160.00 is the level to watch, as the Ministry of Finance could really move the pin on the numerous threats of an FX intervention. Watch reactions as we regain the January highs.

USD/JPY 1H Chart and Technical Levels

Despite the staggering run in the pair, some profit-taking is unrolling just after the pair wicked above January highs to 159.420. Reversing around the mid-line of the textbook bull channel, particularly at current trading levels could bring interesting mean-reversion opportunities.

If the pair continues back above 159.50 and makes a 4H close above, the idea would get pushed back until 160.00, with potential intervention coming up at these levels.

Levels to place on your USD/JPY charts:

  • Dec highs major pivot 157.40 to 157.65
  • 156.00 pivotal support
  • 153.50 to 154.00 minor support
  • 146.00 August range main support

Resistance Levels:

  • Daily and 2026 highs: 159.420
  • 159.00 to 159.50 2026 major resistance
  • April 2024: 160.00 to 160.40 major resistance
  • June 2024 mini resistance 160.70 to 161.00

data.title: Kamada's Pipeline And Biosimilars Set Stage For Future Earnings Expansion
data.description:
Thesis

Kamada Ltd. (KMDA) has just reported a 4Q25 GAAP EPS of $0.06, a figure that misses consensus by about $0.02, while revenue, which hit $44.68 million and represents 14.6% year-over-year growth, also fell short of estimates by about $1.94 million. So shares closed about 4.5% lower on the day of the call. As for outlook, management reaffirmed its 2026 guidance for $200 to $205 million in revenue and $50 to $53 million in adjusted EBITDA. The big news came in the form of a cash dividend announcement of $0.25 per share. This puts the total at about $14.4 million and payable on April 6th next month. It will be for shareholders of record as of March 23rd, just to keep in mind. This is actually a new company's annual cash dividend policy, in which they’re set to distribute at least 50% of annual net income to shareholders. Since my last coverage, the stock has seen about a 7% upside despite the small sell-off post-earnings.

So my case for Kamada stock to see potential upside is via their core plasma-derived products. We also want to see more long-term contracts, as with Kedrab, through 2031. The biosimilars/distribution business could also offer a lot of upside if we see international expansion ramp up. I do, however, see some near-term headwinds, such as competition for Cytogam, as I'll explain. Which is why I've chosen to remain hold rated on the stock.

FY25 earnings

On earnings, we saw Kamada deliver some pretty strong revenue expansion as well as improved profitability. Total revenue managed to reach $180.5 million, which was a 12% year-over-year increase from the $161.0 million we saw back in 2024. We can put the growth mainly down to increased demand for some of the company’s plasma-derived therapies, particularly Varizig and Kedrab, in the U.S. As for international markets, Kamrab and Glassia also did quite well. We also saw the company benefit from expansion in their distribution segment, particularly with the launch of biosimilar and specialty products. Profitability also improved significantly alongside the revenue growth, with gross profit hitting $76.4 million and adjusted EBITDA increasing about 23% to $42.0 million.

So there’s much improved operating leverage here. Net income also climbed, about 40% year-over-year, to $20.2 million, or $0.35 per diluted share. This goes to show decent operating income and favorable financial adjustments compared with the previous year. Now, even with the increased commercial activity, operating expenses stayed relatively stable at $50.2 million. I think the reduced R&D spending, especially after the discontinuation of the Phase 3 inhaled AAT clinical trial, helped offset some higher administrative costs associated with the expanding commercial operations.

As for cash, the company still retained a pretty strong liquidity position and capital structure. Cash generated from operating activities hit $25.5 million in FY25, while year-end cash and cash equivalents managed to reach $75.5 million. However, operating cash flow actually declined compared with 2024; but the change was primarily attributed to higher working capital requirements from growth in receivables and inventory. So the balance sheet still remains rather robust, with total assets of about $378.7 million and shareholders’ equity of $269.1 million. As for guidance, management reaffirmed their 2026 guidance range of $200 to $205 million in revenue to fall in the range of $50 to $53 million in adjusted EBITDA. This would imply continued double-digit growth.

Portfolio performance

A pretty big driver of performance in 2025, and also what investors want to see grow in 2026, was the core commercial products. Particularly, the likes of Kedrab, Glassia, and Cytogam, even though trends somewhat differed across the portfolio. If we start with Kedrab, the company’s rabies immunoglobulin therapy, this is still their largest revenue contributor, and brought in about $54 million in sales during 2025. They have a distribution partnership with Kedrion Biopharma. Demand here is staying pretty strong, with a well-established position in the U.S. rabies post-exposure prophylaxis market.

The company also has long-term contractual visibility to help in this area, with minimum purchase commitments of $90 million for 2026 to 2027. Management also told us there's a supply agreement extending through to 2031, which should give significant revenue stability. As for Glassia, Kamada’s alpha-1 antitrypsin therapy for genetic emphysema, revenue for the product hit $35 million. I think Glassia is seeing a lot of benefits from both direct international product sales and royalty income from sales in the U.S./Canada. As you know, they have a partnership with Takeda Pharmaceutical Company.

However, the flip side was that sales of Cytogam, which is an immunoglobulin therapy used to prevent cytomegalovirus infections in transplant patients, actually declined during the year. Now, it could be the case that clinicians are increasingly utilising newer antiviral therapies. We have to remember that the therapy has to compete with Letermovir and Maribavir post-expanded insurance coverage and improved market access. I think what this comes down to is that they’re much easier to administer and have gained broader insurance coverage. You see, Letermovir and Maribavir are specifically designed to prevent or treat cytomegalovirus (CMV) infections in transplant patients. They’ve also shown very strong clinical efficacy.

So what we’re seeing is insurers expanding reimbursement and hospitals gaining better access to these antivirals. Thus, physicians are probably choosing them more frequently as first-line preventive therapies, which is a big headwind for immunoglobulin treatments like Cytogam. Management's view is that this headwind can be mitigated somewhat. Particularly via a comprehensive post-market research program. This would include the SHIELD clinical study, which is basically designed to evaluate the therapy’s role alongside antivirals in preventing late CMV infection in high-risk kidney transplant recipients.

Valuation appears discounted

As for the valuation, I feel Kamada is trading at pretty reasonable levels relative to their growth profile and sector peers. The company currently trades at about 2.6x EV to sales and 11x EV to EBITDA on a trailing basis. Both of these ratios would be below the healthcare sector medians of about 3.6x and 13.8x, respectively. So that tells me the stock is quite modestly discounted relative to peers, even though we’re seeing them deliver double-digit revenue growth. On an earnings basis, the company trades at around 25x trailing price to earnings, which is broadly in line with the sector, but the consensus estimates would imply a drop to about 19.6x forward price to earnings for 2026. So there are clearly expectations for continued earnings expansion. Overall, the consensus EPS growth is projected at just above 30% in 2026, with the company’s PEG ratio of roughly 0.8 to 1.0, indicating that the market is not actually fully pricing in its projected growth trajectory.

I think the best way to look at it here is that there’s a modest valuation discount reflecting a few upcoming events. The first of these is the recent earnings and revenue miss in the latest quarter. The discontinuation of the inhaled AAT Phase 3 program and the reliance we’re seeing on a concentrated portfolio of plasma-derived specialty therapies would also be a factor here. As you know, this can introduce some regulatory, supply, and reimbursement risks.

Looking ahead: Biosimilars market

A big talking point on the call in terms of future growth was the biosimilars and distribution segment. As I mentioned, they hold a pretty concentrated portfolio of plasma-derived specialty therapies. So this would be a nice diversification strategy for Kamada here. What we know is that the company has already launched two biosimilar products in Israel from 2024 to 2025 and plans to introduce two more biosimilars in 2026. The goal here is to widen the distribution portfolio in the local pharmaceutical market. Biosimilars are basically just lower-cost versions of biologic drugs whose patents have expired, so we shouldn’t see an extensive R&D bill to develop them. We can expect this biosimilar portfolio to generate about $15 to $20 million in annual sales within the next four to five years. I think that appears quite reasonable given the company already has the distribution infrastructure/relationships in place. Now, this figure might still be a pretty small portion of Kamada’s current $180.5 million total revenue base, but keep in mind that their distribution segment only generated about $24 million in 2025. So adding in the biosimilars could eventually account for a large share of that segment.

So, in conclusion, Kamada has shown us a pretty solid operational and financial performance for FY25. There was decent growth in their core plasma-derived products along with a healthy cash position. The dividend policy is another sign of clear upside. However, there are some near-term headwinds, such as competition for Cytogam. The overall concentrated nature of their portfolio could add some investor caution. On the flip side, the expansion into biosimilars could potentially help here, and with the expected ramp-up in international distribution growth, we need to see continued revenue and EBITDA growth in 2026 for more conviction here. That's why I'm maintaining a hold.

data.title: Calumet's Low-Cost Model Takes Hold
data.description:
The messy marketplace Calumet Specialty Products (CLMT) endured over the past few years wasn't wasted time. Management took full advantage where others might have simply marched in place, hoping and waiting. The company spent its resources and time in cost cutting, market honing, and/or process understanding, followed by development. We judge managers by their ability to control what they can. Others may choose a different definition, such as total success; again, we don't.

Now, perhaps there are some hidden surprises to investigate. Thinking about hidden surprises, paper bags are interesting toting containers and make for an interesting analogy. One can't see, unless you're Superman, what's inside until you open the top or rip it apart and peek. Paper bags are easy to open. Investors often miss major opportunities in investing simply because they can't or won't take the time to view the easy. For Calumet, it is really quite simple: just open the top and pull out the investments. Shall we go grab that bag and carefully open it, or is ripping more fun?

This continues our coverage of Calumet, the last being Calumet: Specialty Products' Businesses Coming Together Financially. In the article, we wrote about marketplace changes that support future positive cash flows, including changes in RVO, high specialty margins, and a startup of higher SAF capacity at MRL. We continue monitoring and updating progress with these changes. The results aren't small but rather transformative.

2025 Results

We begin with last year. The December quarter results included the 2025 summary. The results are listed in the next table along with 24 for comparison.

EBITDA (Millions) 25 24
Specialty $292 $223
Performance Brands $48 $57
MRL (Renewables) -$1 $25
Asphalt $33 -$3
*Bonus MRL PTC $8
Corporate -$78
Totals $301 $229

* After the year ended, Calumet was informed that by law it generated an additional $8 million in PTC for 2025.

Accomplishments for 2025 include:

  • An EBITDA increase of $72 million year over year.
  • Breakeven for MRL in what was the most difficult marketplace.
  • Decreased leverage from 8x to 5x within a very difficult renewable production year.
  • Reduced debt by $220 million.
  • Eliminated through refinancing all 26 and 27 debt maturities.
  • Closed a DOE loan, removing $80 million in interest costs.
  • Reduced fixed costs by $80 million:
    • $19 million in transportation.
    • Lowered water costs in Montana by $20 million.
    • Reduced fixed costs by $40 million.
  • Increase production by 1.3 million barrels.
  • Monetized almost $90 in tax credits at MRL. (Basically represents all that was produced.)

The source for the higher EBITDA resides with cost-cutting. Although management isn't expecting another year of $80 million or more in cost savings, they do expect some level of this to continue in 2026. Over the past three years, Calumet spent significant capital toward cost cutting and improving reliability, especially during inclement weather. This capital spent defined the improvement.

Perhaps the most important results were in the cost reduction for renewable diesel (RND) shown in the next slide, lower right.

Operating costs for renewable products dropped in eight quarters from just under $1.10 to $0.42 per gallon. A lot of work and process learning took place to achieve this state-of-the-art level. Long-term averages for the RND margin tend to range above $1.50, reaching toward $2.00. For 2025, the RND wasn't close. An indicator generated by Valero (VLO), DGD, reported an average of $0.6 in 2025, the lowest ever. At times, the DGD fell under $0.3. MRL managed to break even while many operating facilities shuttered.

We argued that the paper bag hiding Calumet's surprise was really quite simple. An example follows with comments to a question at the last conference call by Bruce Fleming, Executive Vice President of Montana. Answering Alexa Petrick of Goldman Sachs, he stated

[T]he regulatory uncertainty as a lot of us call it, is just a feature of the landscape. The global energy transition is a regulated market, but it's collective governments, many, many governments acting directionally. And we feel like that's a very robust framework. We also feel like it adds the equivalent of a lot of margin volatility on top of kind of the base energy. So with that said, if you want to survive in that environment, be a low-cost provider, be well positioned, be able to shift gears quickly, and we think we are all 3.

Notice the last comment: "We think we are all 3." We find it interesting that Darling Ingredients (DAR) chose the opposite approach: long supply chains at fixed contracted costs. In marketplaces with significant volatility, that opposite model doesn't fit in our view. The value of this model plus signed SAF contracts means future cash flows could drastically increase.

2026 & Beyond

For the entire 2026 year, the bag is mixed, with the 2nd half extremely positive. We begin with MRL. Continuing with Fleming's statements, Calumet chooses to "serve in Illinois, Minnesota, the Rockies, Canada, the Pacific Northwest, and California." This creates marketplace diversity, assembling a strategy similar to specialty markets, a concept that Calumet is both familiar with and an expert at implementing. Again, a simple concept defines the direction. Next, the 45Z issue, EPA's target production plus manufacturing rules, is about to be resolved. We included a slide illustrating the value of the change.

The proposed RVO, shown in the top right, increases the values to a level that will require high-cost producers, near $2.00, to reenter. A $2.00 RND margin guarantees Calumet approximately $1.60 for RND profits. At 175 million gallons per year (11,500 barrels per day), the cash flow equals $300 million on a yearly basis. But the SAF premium of $1.50 adds to the 150 million gallons produced, or $225 million in cash. MRL will be targeted at a $500 million-plus per year run rate. We believe that the yearly fixed costs are in the $140 million range. Thus the rounded net cash could equal $400 million. The capital costs, mostly paid by a DOE loan, are about $25 million.

Another interesting statement from the call is also worth reviewing. Louis Borgmann, CEO, stated:

We don't publish it, but I'll tell you that we capture more than 100% of the renewable diesel index margin. And that's because of our ability to shift gears quickly. Now it's worth noting the fourth quarter managed to hit the lowest renewable diesel index margin ever recorded in the history of the world.

A calculation seems in order. For the 4th quarter, MRL showed a loss of $7 million with production at 11,890 barrels per day. The average DGD predictor was $0.61. MRL costs were $0.42. With the 4th quarter days of operation at 92, the DGD predictor difference calculates at $0.19 times 92 times 42 times 11,890, equaling $8.8 million. SAF adds $1.5 times 12,500,000, equaling $18 million. Adding the two together, the cash flow was likely near $27 million. We have extracted from past calculations that the fixed expenses are in the $35 million-plus range, which suggests that the MRL margin is at or maybe a few cents higher than the DGD.

The coming operational changes, SAF-150, require a 48-day outage that began on March 1st and ends in the middle of the 3rd week of April. Unfortunately, two quarters will be negatively affected. For the March quarter at 12,000 barrels per day for 71 days, of which 9.5 million is SAF, cash flow might equal ($1.26 minus $0.41) times 59 times 42 times 12,000 plus (9.5 million gallons of SAF times $1.5) minus $30-$35 million, or $10 million plus or minus. The $10 million March quarterly results might be very low, with the company selling out inventory into March.

Calculating the June quarter is much more difficult. We understand that the plant won't operate at full rates at startup. With a few assumptions, we offer our best guess. The plant could again operate at full rates by the first of May, operating for 61 days. A slide from a past conference shows the targets.

The 2nd quarter calculations might equal (61 days times 9,000 barrels times 42 times $1.5) plus (12,000 barrels times 42 times 61 days times ($1.75 minus $0.42)) minus $30-$35 million in fixed costs, or approximately $45 million.

The rest of the year could be estimated at (($1.75 - $0.42) times 12,000 times 183 days times 42) plus ($1.50 times 75 million) minus $65 million fixed costs, or $170 million. The total for the year might equal $250 million rounded.

Management often discussed low-hanging fruit within the older businesses. We understand that these were delayed. The statement by the CFO - "Despite this, we expect total company production to increase year-over-year on the reliability improvements implemented over the past few years" - hints to us that some of the low fruit is coming with the aggressive capital spending schedule for 2026. In the call, a capital plan of approximately $125 was presented, with every plant targeted for a turnaround.

In more detail, Calumet announced its capital expenses for 2026 at near $125 million ($115 - $145 million), some of which will be paid for by the interest-less DOE loan. Major turnarounds for each major site are planned.

Management also stated that "we expect the site Asphalt at Great Falls to continue producing in the $30 million to $50 million of EBITDA range." This is slightly higher than last year, at the high end $20 million higher. The CEO also stated that the WCS (Western Canadian Crude) spread widened significantly in the past few months: "Further, we are seeing a widening of the WCS differential into 2026."

Looking at 2026 in aggregate, the total EBITDA might equal $325 million from the old business plus $250 million from MRL, or $575 million. The total expenses for interest ($150 million) and capital (net cash to MRL of $110 million), the net cash flow might equal $315 million.

After the 2026 MRL startup, the company will likely add details for the next expansion in 2028. The next slide from the DOE conference in January 2025 summarizes the targets. Originally, the cost was $200 million with a schedule in 2028. The next slide summarizes the MAXSAF project targets.

The most important number on the slide is the increase in production capacity from the 12K barrel per day range to near 25K, or almost double of today. This is huge in that the relatively high fixed costs will be diluted in half. The 300 million gallons of SAF target is also a game changer with respect to financial performance. The extra 150 million SAF product premium might generate an additional $250 million a year in cash without significant fixed cost increases. The 9.5K additional renewable production might add an additional $200 million. Cash flow might step change to near $800–$900 million per year, or $10 plus per share. A different concept with this project drives the implementation; it will be piecemeal. We are expecting more information in the 3rd quarter report. The actual operational learning from the 150 million SAF project will drive final solutions.

A 2027 Estimate

Before continuing, an estimate for 2027 is worthwhile. A table follows with the summary.

EBITDA Estimate (Millions) Specialty PB * Asphalt MRL ** Costs Total
2027 $290 $55 $50 $320 -$80 $625-$650

* Performance Brands

** A calculation equals (($1.75 - $0.40) times 12,000 times 365 days times 42) plus (140 million gallons times $1.50) minus $140 million in fixed costs.

Interest costs plus capital, reduced by $35 million in 2027, equals about $225 million. Cash flow for 2027 might equal approximately $425 million.

In the next year and a half, Calumet might generate $700 million–$750 million in cash. 2029 could add an additional $850 million. We aren't sure what 2028 will be like without more information on the SAFMAX project, but it will be in the $300-plus million range. By the end of 2029, it is more than possible that the cash generated will equal the total debt, including the DOE loans. What will management decide on this kind of cash usage?

Management Scorecard

Management set goals for 2025 including:

  • First, we needed to demonstrate that our Specialties business would consistently generate durable free cash flow amidst large market uncertainty.
  • Second, Montana Renewables needed to prove stand-alone financial resilience and a structural advantage.
  • Third, we needed to receive the transformative DOE loan at Montana Renewables, and last, we accomplished material deleveraging of the balance sheet.

When evaluating each of these objectives, management did achieve the target. At times, the approach was a little awkward. For example, linking a new stock issue valued at up to $65 million with debt extinguishing was one. The market did not appreciate the approach of selling off the stock.

Specialty did perform well within the marketplace. The sales group was able to hold specialty margins above $60, a nice accomplishment in a mid-cycle environment. Performance Brands continues to generate nice returns. For the month of February this year, TRUFUEL beat an old monthly record in sales by over 10%. We rate management quite high with its ability to manage what it can.

Risk

For Calumet, the big risk is that all the eggs are in one basket, being Great Falls. MRL is the future, and it contains the one-site risk. Yes, the company has insurance, but a major stock price hit will occur with a major shutdown event at Great Falls. Also, the EPA still hasn't granted complete small refinery relief. A RIN obligation still technically exists, although the company has never paid a penny for RINs.

Summary

The low-cost structure at MRL the superb execution in the mature businesses offer investors a unique opportunity. The cash flow for 2026 might be $300 million, or $3.5 per share. Run rates for 2027 will be higher because this year only includes one half of a year for MRL 150. 2027 might reach $5 per share.

Next, we included a chart of Calumet derived from Yahoo Finance.

When viewing the chart, several key prices appear. The first is at $30, the second is at $40, and the third is at $50. The low price was very close to $0, close enough to consider $0 in calculating coming targets. A break of $30 puts $60 in play, a break of $40 puts $80 in play, and a break of $50 puts $100 in play. Often times, technical analysis misses the value in very long-term charts. This might be an example of that, believing that 20-year charting doesn't matter. Our experience suggests that they do matter more often than not.

The question becomes, will fundamental earnings support the technical values? We believe so, in that with SAFMAX and its 300 million gallons per year of SAF, the cash flow per share at 85 million shares equals more than $10. Thus, we rate the stock a strong buy on weakness under $30 and a buy above $30.

So what was in the paper bag? It contained a lot of cash. By 2028, the yearly paper bag cash could be above $1 billion per year.

data.title: Santacruz Silver: Growth Catalyst And Undervaluation Make It A Cautious Buy
data.description:
Santacruz Silver Mining Analysis

I spend a lot of time screening silver producers, and Santacruz Silver Mining (SCZM) wasn't a name I had on my watchlist until recently, as I've been more focused on larger silver miners (SIL). But here's a company producing over 14 million silver equivalent ounces per year across five operations, with big production growth upside, generating real cash flow, and trading at a valuation that would look cheap even if silver traded $20-$30 lower.

The company got a recent boost when it started trading on NASDAQ in January and also recently ranked first on the 2026 TSX Venture 50 list, based on its stock price performance. I think the stock is a buy here for several reasons.

Santacruz: The Multi-Asset Advantage

One thing I like about Santacruz is the diversification you get from owning the stock. The Zimapan mine in Mexico is the largest contributor at 3.98 million silver equivalent ounces in 2025. In Bolivia, the company operates the Bolivar mine (2.37 Moz AgEq), Caballo Blanco group (2.78 Moz AgEq), Porco mine (1.38 Moz AgEq), and the San Lucas ore feed sourcing business (3.88 Moz AgEq).

Overall, investors get a diversified asset base with four producing mines in two countries, reducing single-mine and single-country risk that can crush investors in this sector. I also like that it has a diverse mix of metals - silver, zinc, lead, and copper.

Q3 2025: Resilience, Despite The Bolivar Setback

The Q3 2025 results weren't great. But I think that they serve as a reminder that mining can sometimes throw you curveballs.

Here's what happened: A flooding event at Bolivar in May 2025 restricted access to the high-grade Pomabamba and Nané zones, gutting silver production at that mine. Silver output at Bolivar ended up falling 57% quarter-over-quarter and 73% year-over-year.

Mine AgEq Produced QoQ Change (AgEq) Silver Zinc Tonnes Milled
Bolivar (100% basis) 420,612 -30% 132,146 3,186 52,023
Porco (100% basis) 318,694 -12% 92,001 2,488 49,161
Caballo Blanco 707,465 +3% 294,524 4,131 62,221
San Lucas Group 986,403 +5% 326,873 7,032 100,550
Zimapan 991,643 +3% 396,385 4,744 222,630
Consolidated 3,424,817 -3% 1,241,929 21,581 486,585

But I think it's important to note that context matters. Bolivar's production is reported on a 100% basis, while Santacruz books only 45% of revenues and expenses through its NPI agreement with COMIBOL.

More importantly, the rest of the portfolio stepped up. Caballo Blanco, Zimapan, and San Lucas all delivered a strong quarter, and that's why you see production relatively stable from the prior quarter. Again, this is why it's so crucial to have diversification in this sector.

CEO Arturo Préstamo noted in the Q3 release that the company paid off its final installment to Glencore for the Bolivian asset acquisition during the quarter, a great move. This removes a significant balance sheet liability while still maintaining $69.2 million in working capital.

Recovery at Bolivar is progressing on schedule, with the high-grade zones expected to return to service progressively starting in February 2026 and reach full recovery by Q4 2026.

Q4 2025: The Recovery Is Underway

AgEq Production by Mine

Mine Q3 2025 Q4 2025 QoQ Change
Bolivar (100% basis) 420,612 565,694 +34%
Porco (100% basis) 318,694 330,176 +4%
Caballo Blanco 707,465 730,108 +3%
San Lucas 986,403 1,095,945 +11%
Zimapan 991,643 1,017,096 +3%
Consolidated 3,424,817 3,739,019 +9%

The most recent Q4 2025 production release tells you a lot about where this company is headed. Consolidated production came in at 3.74 million silver equivalent ounces, up 9% sequentially from Q3's 3.42 million. Every operation improved. That matters.

The Bolivar recovery is key to the investment thesis for Santacruz. Silver equivalent production there jumped 34% quarter-over-quarter to 565,694 ounces, with silver output up 53% as head grades climbed from 89 g/t back to 108 g/t. Yes, it's well below the pre-flood levels north of 200 g/t, but the trend is obviously going in the right direction. Throughput rose 22% as access to the Pomabamba and Nané veins improved. Management reiterated they expect full recovery by Q4 2026, so I expect better results later this year.

The rest of the portfolio kept humming. Caballo Blanco posted 730,108 AgEq ounces (+3% QoQ). San Lucas was the standout at 1.10 million AgEq ounces, up 11% on higher throughput and better grades. Zimapan and Porco both ticked up modestly.

Full-year 2025 production landed at 14.4 million silver equivalent ounces, down 11% from 2024's 16.2 million. That decline is almost entirely the Bolivar flood. Strip it out, and the rest of the portfolio was essentially flat year-over-year despite lower grades at Porco.

Q4 earnings are expected May 6, and that report will be the first time we see the full-year cost picture and whether AISC started trending back down in the second half.

Soracaya: The Growth Catalyst

Where does the investor upside come from with this stock? I think the answer to that question is Santacruz's Soracaya project in Bolivia.

This brownfield development hosts 34.5 million ounces of silver in resources plus significant zinc and lead credits. While it only has inferred resources, which are considered more speculative than measured and indicated resources, management is being aggressive and targeting permitting by Q3 2026 and initial production by Q4 2026.

If they deliver, it would be a boost to the stock. It adds meaningful ounces to an already sizable production base without the years-long construction cycle that most development projects require.

Valuation: Looks Reasonable After A Big Run

C$34.20 / ~US$25*

*Note: Santacruz pays this analyst, Atrium Research, C$3,500 per month for its services, which include research reports, as noted in this 2025 press release.

At roughly 4.8x the lone analyst's 2026 EPS estimate, Santacruz trades at a steep discount to just about every silver producer I cover. Hecla Mining (HL) is at 32x forward earnings. First Majestic (AG) north of 20x. Even Silvercorp Metals (SVM), which I recently covered as a Buy, trades around 6-8x forward earnings.

But, the lack of analyst coverage is clearly a detriment. One company-sponsored analyst (Atrium Research) isn't the institutional validation that moves the needle for larger investors. The NASDAQ listing should help address that over time.

The Risks Are Real

AISC trajectory. This is the biggest concern. Q3 AISC of $35.62/oz was a 30% jump year-over-year, driven by higher ore purchase costs at San Lucas, Bolivar remediation spending, and plant improvements at Zimapan. The Bolivian boliviano's appreciation against the dollar also pushed up costs. Management says year-to-date AISC remains in the mid-$20s, but the direction of travel needs to reverse. Watch Q4 results on May 6 (estimated earnings date) very closely.

Bolivia jurisdiction risk. The Bolivar and Porco mines operate under NPI agreements with COMIBOL, where Santacruz receives only 45% of profits. Bolivia is a solid place to do business, but it is technically not a Tier 1 mining jurisdiction like the U.S., Canada, and Australia. Political dynamics, regulatory changes, and currency movements all represent real risks.

Bolivar recovery timeline. Management expects full recovery by Q4 2026, but mine flooding remediation is inherently uncertain. Any delays would weigh on production and keep AISC elevated longer than expected.

Bottom Line

Santacruz Silver is not a "safe" stock, and I think there are lower-risk silver producers to gain silver exposure—namely, the larger silver miners like Pan American Silver (PAAS) and Hecla, and even Silvercorp Metals likely carry less risk than Santacruz. After all, it's a small-cap (sub-$1 billion market cap), thinly covered silver producer operating in Bolivia and Mexico with elevated costs and a flooding recovery still in progress.

But the setup is interesting here, priced under $10 per share. You're getting 14-15 million ounces of annual silver equivalent production at a reasonable valuation and a growth catalyst in Soracaya that could add meaningful production by late 2026. With silver near $87/oz, as of writing, the upside potential more than compensates for the risk.

I'm initiating coverage with a cautious Buy rating, with a preference for building a position on pullbacks below $10. Q4 earnings on May 6 will be the next major data point. Keep a close eye on cash costs and capital expenditures.

data.title: Putnam Strategic Intermediate Municipal Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: In the fourth quarter of 2025, new issuance of municipal (muni) bonds exceeded the average of recent years, contributing to a full-year total 13% above last year's figure. Muni to US Treasury (UST) ratios remained stable during the period. By quarter-end, the yield on the benchmark 10-year UST increased by two basis points (bps) to 4.17%, while the 30-year UST yield increased by 11 bps to 4.84%. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) closed the year with three consecutive 25-bp rate cut that was well received by markets, but the future path of rate cuts is unclear as Fed members differ regarding their assessment of near-term inflation and growth projections.
  • Contributors: Overweight allocation to bonds with no external credit rating and to bonds with 20 or more years to maturity. Security selection in A and BB rated bonds.
  • Detractors: Security selection in AA rated bonds and overweight allocation to BB rated bonds.
  • Outlook: Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and preserving portfolio quality.

Performance Review

  • Over the quarter, Putnam Strategic Intermediate Municipal Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg 3-15 Year Blend Municipal Bond Index.
  • The fund's yield curve positioning contributed to relative performance during the fourth quarter, primarily driven by our overweight to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity. However, our underweight to muni bonds with two to five years to maturity also contributed to returns.
  • Rating allocations benefited relative returns for the period, led by our overweight to bonds with no external credit rating, while an overweight to BB rated bonds curbed returns. Security selection detracted from relative results, particularly due to selection in AA and BBB rated securities. Meanwhile selection in A and BB rated securities added to returns.

Outlook

  • While Q4 muni bond issuance exceeded historical averages, the pace of issuance slowed going into year-end, nonetheless full-year issuance exceeded last year's already elevated levels. Looking ahead to 2026, demand will play a crucial role as supply is projected to reach exceptional highs, primarily driven by infrastructure capital requirements that can no longer depend on previous government stimulus programs alone. This provides a stronger technical backdrop as demand has also increased over the past several months. The muni-bond yield curve remained relatively stable during the quarter.
  • Fundamentals remain stable; however, slower economic growth will limit expansion in revenues across many sectors, in particular tax revenues. Rainy day funds remain healthy at the state and local government levels, which bolsters fiscal resilience despite ongoing expense growth.
  • Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. Yields remain above five-year averages, allowing investors to lock in compelling income. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and preserving portfolio quality.
